content.body: A polynomial is just a sum of power terms, like $x^2 + x$. The good news: the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives. You can differentiate each term on its own and add the results — that's the sum rule.
So a whole polynomial breaks into a few easy power-rule problems. For $x^2 + x$:
Differentiate term by term. Drag the correct derivative of each term into the blanks to build $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3 + x^2$. Since it's a sum, the order doesn't matter.

sandbox.caption: Walk through the power rule in its general form (a·xⁿ → a·n·xⁿ⁻¹), then apply that same move to each term of this polynomial in turn.

solution.body: The sum rule lets you differentiate each term on its own:
Add the pieces: $f'(x) = 3x^2 + 2x$. Drag in the $3x^2$ and $2x$ tiles — for a sum the order doesn't matter.

feedback.correct: Right — $\frac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2$ and $\frac{d}{dx}(x^2) = 2x$, so the derivative is $3x^2 + 2x$.
feedback.incorrect: Differentiate each term separately with the power rule, then add. You need both $3x^2$ and $2x$ (in either order).
feedback.hint: Apply the power rule to each term on its own — bring the exponent down and drop it by one — then drag in the two results.

content.body: Differentiate term by term, then evaluate. For $f(x) = x^3 - 2x$, what is $f'(2)$?

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general symbolic form first, then differentiate each term the same way before substituting your point.

solution.body: Differentiate term by term: $x^3 \to 3x^2$ and $-2x \to -2$, so

feedback.hint: You have $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$ — now evaluate it at $x = 2$.

content.body: Two more details and you can handle any polynomial. Coefficients ride along — multiply by them after applying the power rule — and the derivative of a constant is 0, so plain numbers drop away.
Term by term: $2x^3 \to 6x^2$, $4x^2 \to 8x$, $-x \to -1$, and the $+7$ vanishes.
Put it all together. What is the derivative of $f(x) = 2x^3 + 4x^2 - x + 7$?

sandbox.caption: Replay the power rule in general form — multiply by the exponent, then drop it by one — and use it on every term of this polynomial.

solution.body: Differentiate each term, carrying coefficients and dropping the constant:

feedback.correct: Yes — each term differentiates on its own, and the constant 7 becomes 0.
feedback.incorrect: Apply the power rule to each term, and remember the derivative of 7 is 0.
feedback.hint: Hit each term with the power rule, carrying its coefficient, and let the constant $7$ fall away.

content.body: Here is $f(x) = x^2 + x$, whose derivative is $f'(x) = 2x + 1$. Find the value of $x$ where the tangent slope equals 5, then drag the slider there.

sandbox.caption: Review the power rule in general form, then apply it term by term to build the slope formula you'll work with here.

solution.body: The derivative is $f'(x) = 2x + 1$. Set it equal to the target slope, 5:
Drag the slider to $x = 2$, where the tangent slope is 5.

phases.text: Drag to $x = 2$, where the tangent slope is 5

feedback.correct: Nice — $2x + 1 = 5$ gives $x = 2$, and the tangent there has slope 5.
feedback.incorrect: Set the slope formula equal to 5: $2x + 1 = 5$. Solve for $x$, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: You have the slope formula $2x + 1$ — set it equal to 5 and solve that for $x$.

content.body: That's all there is to differentiating polynomials: hit each term with the power rule, carry the coefficients, drop the constants, and add. With the sum rule, even a long polynomial is just a handful of one-line steps.
Next, you'll use derivatives to read the shape of a graph — where it rises, falls, and turns.
Assemble $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3 + x^2$ by dragging a tile into each blank.

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form (a·xⁿ → a·n·xⁿ⁻¹), then run each term of this polynomial through the same move.

feedback.incorrect: Differentiate each term on its own with the power rule, then drag both pieces in.
feedback.hint: Differentiate each term with the power rule — bring the exponent down and lower it by one.

content.body: Build $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 2x^3 + 5x$. Drag a tile into each blank.

sandbox.caption: Practice the general power rule first — bring the exponent down in front and lower it by one — then apply it to each term separately.

feedback.incorrect: Bring down each exponent as a coefficient, and remember $5x$ differentiates to a constant.
feedback.hint: Use the power rule on $2x^3$, and note that a linear term like $5x$ differentiates to a constant.

content.body: Assemble $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^4 - 3x^2$. Mind the minus sign between the terms.

sandbox.caption: Walk through the power rule in general form, then differentiate each term and carry the sign that sits between them.

feedback.incorrect: Differentiate each term, then keep the subtraction so the second term stays negative.
feedback.hint: Apply the power rule to each term, and carry the minus sign so the second term stays negative.

content.body: Match each function to its derivative.

sandbox.caption: Step through the general power rule, then use it term by term on each function before pairing it with its match.

feedback.correct: Great — differentiating each function term by term lines them up perfectly.
feedback.incorrect: Apply the power rule to each term of every function, then find its match.
feedback.hint: Differentiate each function one term at a time, then pair it with that derivative.

content.body: Build $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3 + 7$. Watch what happens to the constant.

sandbox.caption: Replay the power rule in general form, applying it to each term — and recall that a constant term contributes nothing.

feedback.correct: Yes — $x^3 \to 3x^2$ and the constant $7$ becomes $0$, so $f'(x) = 3x^2$.
feedback.incorrect: A constant term has slope $0$, so it drops away. What's left from $x^3$?
feedback.hint: Use the power rule on $x^3$, and remember the constant $7$ just drops away.

content.body: Use the steppers to build the derivative of $f(x) = 4x^5$.

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form — bring the power down in front, drop the exponent by one, then multiply. Apply the same move to this question's term.

feedback.correct: Exactly — multiply by the exponent ($4 \cdot 5 = 20$) and drop it by one to get $20x^4$.
feedback.incorrect: Power rule: multiply the coefficient by the exponent, then lower the exponent by one.
feedback.hint: Bring the exponent down as a factor on the coefficient, then lower the power by one.

content.body: Put the steps for differentiating a polynomial in the right order.

items: Split the polynomial into separate terms
Differentiate each term with the power rule

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form — it's the move you apply to each term once the polynomial is split apart.

feedback.correct: That's the routine — split the polynomial into terms, differentiate each with the power rule, then add the results back together.
feedback.incorrect: Split the whole polynomial into separate terms first, differentiate each one, and combine them only at the end.
feedback.hint: The power rule works on one term at a time — so what has to happen to the polynomial before you can use it, and what's left after?

content.body: $f(x) = x^2 - 4x$ has $f'(x) = 2x - 4$, which is zero at $x = 2$. Label each interval as increasing or decreasing.

sandbox.caption: Review how the power rule works in general form — the same move that produces the derivative whose sign you'll test here.

feedback.correct: Right — $f' < 0$ left of $x = 2$ (decreasing) and $f' > 0$ to the right (increasing).
feedback.incorrect: Plug a test value from each side of $x = 2$ into $f'(x) = 2x - 4$ and check its sign.
feedback.hint: On each side of $x = 2$, test whether $f'(x) = 2x - 4$ comes out negative or positive.

content.body: Match each function to the value of $f'(1)$.

sandbox.caption: Walk through the general power rule, then apply it term by term to each function before evaluating at the given point.

feedback.correct: Nice — differentiate each function, then plug in $x = 1$.
feedback.incorrect: First find $f'(x)$ for each function, then substitute $x = 1$.
feedback.hint: Find $f'(x)$ for each function, then evaluate it at $x = 1$ before matching.

content.body: Assemble $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3 + 2x^2 + x$ by filling each blank.

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form, then apply the same move to each of the three terms here.

feedback.incorrect: Differentiate all three terms; don't forget that $x$ on its own becomes $1$.
feedback.hint: Apply the power rule to all three terms — and don't forget the lone $x$ contributes a term of its own.
